scenes.text:
The road ends at a wall of birches. They are the color of old bone and they have been carved — every trunk, waist-high, a single sigil that you do not know. The wind here smells like a kitchen: rosemary, blood, woodsmoke.

Ash, if he rode this far with you, will not cross. He says the horses won't either. He says it like a man stating the weather.

A crow drops a tooth at your boot. Human. Adult. Clean at the root.

choices.text: Walk the tree line, looking for a way around.

scenes.text:
The tooth is warm. That is the part you will think about, later, when you cannot sleep.

You pocket it because leaving it felt worse. The crow watches you do it and makes a small, satisfied sound, like a woman clearing her throat.

"Good," it says, in a voice that is almost a voice. "She likes guests who bring something."

Then it is just a crow again, and it is gone.

You walk the edge for an hour and the edge does not end. The sun moves wrong. When you look back, your own bootprints are pointed the other way.

A man hangs from a low branch by a rope of braided grass. His feet are inches off the moss. He is not dead. He opens one eye when your shadow falls on him.

"Don't," he says, calm. "Cut me down and she'll know. Walk past and I keep counting. I'm at four thousand and eleven."

do: journal:Left a man hanging in the witch's wood. He was counting.

scenes.text:
"Breaths," he says. "Hers. She breathes once a day. When she breathes ten thousand times I'm done."

His tongue is gray. His eyes are clear.

"I came looking for my sister. The Gate took her. The witch said she'd tell me where, for a price. I said yes too fast." He almost smiles. "Tell her no, the first three times. Three. She respects three."

A crow lands on his shoulder and he does not flinch.

The grass-rope parts easy as wet paper. He drops, folds, lies still. For a long second you think you have killed a corpse.

Then he breathes. Once. Loud. And the wood breathes with him.

Every birch around you exhales at the same time and the wind that comes off them is hot and smells of a woman's hair. Somewhere not far, something laughs — not cruelly. Pleased.

The man gets to his feet and runs. He does not look back. The crows go with him.

Inside, the wood is quieter than a chapel. Moss eats the sound of your feet. The light is the color of weak tea.

You pass a deer in a clearing. It stops eating and looks at you. On its head, instead of antlers, is a circle of black iron, beaten thin, set with one red stone. A crown. Old work. Kingly work.

It chews. It watches. It does not run.

choices.text: Wolves with too-clear eyes step out of the green.

scenes.text:
You come close enough to see the deer's eye is brown and wet and human-sad. The crown is welded to the bone. There is a brand on its flank. Three lines and a circle — {other_name}'s seal.

You understand, all at once, what you are looking at.

It makes a sound. A small one. It might be a name. It might be your name.

Then it walks past you, slow, polite, and is gone into the trees.

choices.text: Sit down in the moss for a moment.
do: journal:I think I just saw {other_name}. He had hooves.

scenes.text:
You swing. The deer does not move. The blade goes into its neck like into a sack of wet leaves and the deer looks at you, patient, and does not bleed.

It says, very clearly, in a man's voice: "{other_sibling_cap}. You're early."

You drop the weapon. You are sick into the moss for a long time. The deer is gone when you look up. The crown is on the ground at your feet. It is too small for any human head.

do: journal:I cut him. He spoke. I left the crown.
journal:I took the crown off the thing that used to be a {other_short}.

scenes.text:
The path goes on. Once, you cross a clearing where a leaf falls upward, slow, into a branch that catches it like a hand. A bird flies backwards through it. Time, here, has been hurt.

You keep walking because to stand still feels like being eaten.

After a while there is woodsmoke, real woodsmoke, and the smell of something stewing that has bones in it. A small house. Crooked. Lit warm.

choices.text: Watch the house from the trees first.
Something is moving in the brush — low, fast, in a pack.

scenes.text:
You crouch for a long while. The house breathes smoke. A woman moves past the window — tall, dark-haired, sleeves rolled, forearms streaked to the elbow in something that is not quite blood.

She is humming. The song is one your mother knew.

She stops humming. She turns her face directly to the tree you are behind. She smiles. She has a kind smile.

"Come in," she calls, soft. "The stew's on. I've set a bowl."

The Hedge Witch is younger than the stories. Maybe thirty. Maybe three hundred. Her kitchen smells like every kitchen you have ever loved and one you have not.

She sets a bowl in front of you. Stew. Real stew. "Eat. You're walking on empty and I won't talk to a fainting woman." Or man. Whichever you are, she sees you exactly.

She sits. She waits. She does not eat.

"You want to know about the Gate," she says. "And the thing that lives behind it. And whether your {other_short} and your {missing_short} are meat yet."

choices.text: "I'm not buying. I came to ask."
Sleep at the edge of the wood. Dream.
Ask the witch about her mother in a jar.

scenes.text:
You eat. It is the best meal you have had in a month. There is bread. There is butter the color of a daisy. When you are done she takes the bowl and washes it herself.

"Good," she says. "Now you owe me one small thing. A meal eaten is a meal owed. That is not magic. That is manners."

She smiles. It is still a kind smile. That is the part that frightens you.

choices.text: "I owe you nothing. Speak plain or I leave."

scenes.text:
She tilts her head. "No," she says. "All right."

She does not press. She refills her own cup. She watches you the way a farmer watches a sky.

"Ask again," she says, "if you mean it. Three times is the number that counts in this house."

choices.text: "Tell me about the Keeper." (second refusal of her bargain)

do: journal:Walked out of the witch's house with nothing. The wood took a price anyway.

scenes.text:
"The Keeper is older than the Gate. The Gate is just a door someone built to keep it in." She turns her cup. "It does not want your {missing_short}. It wants the line. Blood that has sat on the chair. It is making itself a new one. A small one. With a crown that fits."

You think of the deer.

"That," she says, watching your face, "is the second answer. The third costs."

choices.text: Refuse her again. Push for the third.

do: journal:The Keeper wants the royal line. Not {missing_title}. The line.

scenes.text:
She laughs, low. "Three. Good. The hanged man taught you well, then."

She reaches across the table and her hand is over yours before you can pull back. Her palm is warm and dry and small. She does not take anything. She just looks at you, level.

"Free, then. The Keeper has a name. Names matter to old things. I will not say it aloud in this house. But I will write it." She does. On the inside of your wrist, in something that is not ink. "Speak it at the Gate. Once. It will hesitate. A hesitation is sometimes a door."

do: journal:She wrote a name on my wrist. I cannot read it. I will know when to say it.

scenes.text:
"Good," she says. "Plain talk. I'll give you sight — a draught. Two swallows. With it you read what is written in dark ink, you see what walks behind a man, you understand the Keeper's library if you get that far. Without it, you'll die a fool in a tower."

She sets a small green bottle between you.

"The price is this. You drink, and a piece of what you see goes into me. Not your soul. Your edges. You'll be a little less sure of yourself afterwards. People will trust you a little less. You'll trust yourself a little less."

do: journal:Drank with the witch. Took the green bottle. Something of me is hers now.
journal:Refused the witch. The wood took a tooth on the way out.

scenes.text:
She walks you to the door. Outside, the sun is the right color again. The wood feels thinner.

"I could come," she says, not looking at you. "Part of the way. I am tired of this house. The Gate is the last interesting thing left and I would like to see how it ends."

She waits. She does not push.

do: journal:The Hedge Witch is walking with me. I am not sure that is good news.

choices.text: "No. Stay here. I'll do this alone."

scenes.text:
You walk out of the house and into the wood and the wood is not the same wood. The light is gray. Your mouth tastes of metal. Somewhere, a thread you could have pulled is being burned, and you can almost smell it.

By nightfall you are back on the king's road, footsore, no wiser. The wind has the city in it now — woodsmoke, old fish, fear.

The trees thin. The road comes back to meet you like a dog that thought you were lost.

Ahead, the country opens. To the west, a thin black needle against a yellow sky — the wizard's tower. To the south, low and smudged, smoke that does not move like cookfire smoke. The city is burning, slow, somewhere in its belly.

You have a choice to make, and the wood does not care which.

choices.text: Help Moth find her crows' tower.
